name: Mediterranean Diet
note: Olive oil, vegetables, legumes, fish, whole grains, nuts, and moderate wine. Gold standard for cardiovascular health, cognitive longevity, and inflammation reduction.

name: Anti-Inflammatory Diet
note: Emphasizes omega-3s, polyphenols, colorful produce, and fiber while eliminating processed foods, refined carbohydrates, and industrial seed oils.

name: Low-FODMAP Diet
note: Restricts fermentable carbohydrates (lactose, fructose, fructans, GOS, polyols). Used for IBS and SIBO symptom management. 6-8 week elimination then structured reintroduction.

name: AIP Protocol
note: Strict elimination removing grains, legumes, dairy, eggs, nightshades, nuts, seeds, alcohol, and NSAIDs. Targets gut healing and immune regulation in autoimmune conditions.

name: Paleo Diet
note: Emphasizes meat, fish, eggs, vegetables, fruit, nuts, and seeds. Excludes grains, legumes, dairy, and processed foods. Modeled on pre-agricultural dietary patterns.

name: Ketogenic Diet
note: Very low carbohydrate (<50g/day), high fat intake inducing ketosis. Used for epilepsy, metabolic syndrome, weight loss, and cognitive conditions. Requires electrolyte monitoring.

name: Low-Histamine Diet
note: Eliminates high-histamine foods (aged cheeses, fermented foods, wine, processed meats, spinach, tomatoes). Used for histamine intolerance and MCAS support.

name: Gluten-Free Diet
note: Strict avoidance of gluten (wheat, barley, rye, and cross-contaminated oats). Required for celiac disease; used therapeutically for non-celiac gluten sensitivity and autoimmune conditions.

name: Dairy-Free Diet
note: Eliminates all cow dairy products. Used for dairy intolerance, casein sensitivity, eczema, acne, and mucus-related conditions. Goat and sheep dairy sometimes tolerated.

name: Elimination Diet
note: Removes the most common food allergens and sensitizers (gluten, dairy, eggs, soy, corn, nuts, shellfish) for 3-4 weeks, then reintroduces one at a time to identify triggers.

name: Low-Oxalate Diet
note: Limits high-oxalate foods (spinach, beets, nuts, chocolate, tea). Used for kidney stone prevention (calcium oxalate stones), vulvodynia, and oxalate sensitivity.

name: Carnivore Diet
note: Restricted to animal foods only (meat, organs, eggs, some dairy). Used for autoimmune conditions, inflammatory bowel disease, and as an extreme elimination protocol.

name: Plant-Based Diet
note: Emphasizes whole plant foods with minimal or no animal products. Benefits for cardiovascular health and metabolic function. Requires attention to B12, iron, zinc, omega-3, and vitamin D.

name: GAPS Diet
note: Removes grains, starchy vegetables, and processed sugars to heal gut permeability. Emphasizes meat stocks, fermented foods, and animal fats. Used for autism, ADHD, and gut dysfunction.

name: Intermittent Fasting (16:8)
note: 16-hour fasting window with 8-hour eating window. Supports insulin sensitivity, metabolic flexibility, autophagy, and weight management. Common example: eat between 12pm and 8pm.

name: Time-Restricted Eating (14:10)
note: 14-hour fasting window with 10-hour eating window. More accessible than 16:8. Aligns eating with circadian rhythm — stop eating 3 hours before bed.

name: Eating Slowly
note: Chew each bite 20-30 times. Allows salivary enzyme activity, improves digestion, enhances satiety signals, and reduces overeating. Particularly helpful for IBS and bloating.

name: Red meat
note: Prioritize grass-fed and pasture-raised sources. Rich in heme iron, B12, zinc, creatine, carnitine, and complete protein. Aim for 2-4 servings per week.

name: Organ meat
note: Especially beef liver — the most nutrient-dense food per calorie. Rich in retinol (vitamin A), B12, folate, iron, copper, CoQ10, and choline. Even 1-2 servings per week is impactful.

name: Eggs
note: Whole eggs preferred over whites. Rich in choline, fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K, lutein, and complete protein. Pasture-raised eggs have superior omega-3 and vitamin D content.

name: Fatty fish
note: Rich in EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D, selenium, and iodine. Aim for 2-3 servings per week. Wild-caught salmon and sardines offer best toxin-to-nutrient ratio.

name: White fish
note: Lean protein source with good iodine, selenium, and B12 content. Lower omega-3 than fatty fish but lower in mercury. Versatile and easy to digest.

name: Shellfish
note: Oysters are exceptionally rich in zinc, copper, B12, and selenium. Shrimp and other shellfish provide lean protein with excellent iodine content. Eat 1-2 times per week.

name: Poultry
note: Lean protein with good B3, B6, and phosphorus content. Thighs have more iron and fat-soluble nutrients than breast meat. Pasture-raised preferred for superior nutrient profile.

name: Bone broth
note: Rich in glycine, proline, collagen peptides, and minerals (calcium, phosphorus, magnesium). Supports gut lining, joint health, and skin. Homemade or quality commercial brands preferred.

name: Full-fat dairy
note: Provides fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K2, conjugated linoleic acid (CLA), and calcium. Whole fat dairy does not increase cardiovascular risk per current evidence. Choose quality sources.

name: Greek yogurt
note: High in protein (17-20g per cup), probiotics, and calcium. Choose plain, unsweetened, full-fat varieties. A5 beta-casein (Jersey cow) or goat yogurt tolerated better by some.

name: Kefir
note: Fermented milk with diverse probiotic cultures (bacteria and yeasts). Higher probiotic diversity than yogurt. Goat kefir tolerated well by those sensitive to cow dairy.

name: Hard cheese
note: Concentrated source of calcium, protein, K2, and fat-soluble vitamins. Aged and fermented varieties often tolerated even with mild lactose intolerance. Limit processed cheese.

name: Butter (grass-fed)
note: Rich in fat-soluble vitamins A and K2, CLA, and butyrate. Grass-fed butter (e.g., from spring pasture) has significantly higher K2 and omega-3 content than conventional butter.

name: Ghee
note: Butter with milk solids and water removed. Shelf-stable and lactose-free. Rich in butyrate, K2, and fat-soluble vitamins. High smoke point makes it ideal for cooking.

name: Leafy greens
note: Aim for 2-3 cups daily. Rich in folate, magnesium, vitamin K1, calcium, and antioxidants. Raw and cooked varieties provide different nutrient bioavailability.

name: Cruciferous vegetables
note: Rich in sulforaphane, indole-3-carbinol, vitamin C, and fiber. Supports detoxification (NRF2 pathway), estrogen metabolism, and cancer prevention. Light cooking preserves myrosinase activity.

name: Root vegetables
note: Nutrient-dense complex carbohydrates. Sweet potato is rich in beta-carotene and potassium. Beets support nitric oxide and liver detoxification. Good resistant starch source when cooled.

name: Alliums
note: Rich in quercetin, allicin, and organosulfur compounds. Supports immune function, cardiovascular health, and prebiotic fiber for gut bacteria. Cook garlic gently to preserve allicin.

name: Nightshade vegetables
note: Nutritious for most people. Rich in lycopene, vitamin C, and antioxidants. Avoided on the AIP protocol due to lectins and solanine. Associated with flares in a subset with autoimmune conditions.

name: Mushrooms
note: One of few non-animal dietary sources of vitamin D (when UV-exposed). Rich in beta-glucans, B vitamins, selenium, and immune-modulating compounds. Cook thoroughly.

name: Sea vegetables
note: Rich in iodine, fucoidan, minerals, and fiber. Supports thyroid function. Consume in moderation — iodine content varies widely and excessive intake can dysregulate thyroid.

name: Berries
note: Highest antioxidant density of any fruit. Rich in anthocyanins, vitamin C, fiber, and polyphenols. Wild blueberries have higher polyphenol content than cultivated varieties.

name: Citrus fruit
note: Rich in vitamin C, folate, and flavonoids (hesperidin, naringenin). Whole fruit preferred over juice for fiber content. Grapefruit can interact with many medications via CYP3A4 inhibition.

name: Avocado
note: Rich in monounsaturated fats, potassium, folate, magnesium, and fiber. Enhances absorption of fat-soluble nutrients from other foods. High satiety value.

name: Banana
note: Good source of potassium, B6, and quick energy. Green (unripe) bananas and cooled cooked bananas provide resistant starch as prebiotic fiber. Higher glycemic when very ripe.

name: Apple
note: Rich in quercetin, pectin (soluble fiber), and malic acid. Eat with skin for polyphenols and fiber. Supports gut microbiome and bile acid recycling.

name: Pomegranate
note: Exceptionally rich in punicalagins and ellagic acid. Supports cardiovascular health, estrogen metabolism, and urolithin A production (mitophagy support). Choose whole seeds or 100% juice.

name: White rice
note: Easy-to-digest, low-antinutrient starch. Well-tolerated during gut healing, IBS, and digestive distress. Lacks fiber of brown rice but significantly easier on sensitive GI systems.

name: Oats
note: Rich in beta-glucan (soluble fiber) for cholesterol reduction and blood sugar balance. Use certified gluten-free for celiac or gluten sensitivity. Contains avenin — avoid on strict AIP.

name: Quinoa
note: Pseudograin with complete protein profile. Gluten-free. Rich in magnesium, iron, and fiber. Rinse well before cooking to remove saponins which can irritate gut lining.

name: Sourdough bread
note: Long fermentation pre-digests gluten and phytic acid, improving tolerability and nutrient bioavailability. Some with non-celiac gluten sensitivity tolerate true sourdough well.

name: Gluten-free grains
note: Options include rice, millet, sorghum, buckwheat, teff, and certified GF oats. Preferable to regular wheat for those with celiac, gluten sensitivity, or inflammatory gut conditions.

name: Olive oil (extra virgin)
note: Rich in oleocanthal (natural anti-inflammatory), oleic acid (monounsaturated fat), and polyphenols. Use raw for maximum polyphenol benefit. Light heat cooking is acceptable.

name: Coconut oil
note: Rich in medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs), particularly lauric acid. Supports ketone production and antimicrobial activity. High saturated fat — use in moderation.

name: Avocado oil
note: High smoke point oil rich in monounsaturated fats and vitamin E. Suitable for high-heat cooking. Neutral flavor. Good alternative to seed oils.

name: Flaxseed oil
note: Highest plant-based source of ALA omega-3. Must be refrigerated and never heated. Add to smoothies or dressings. Do not use for cooking.

name: Fermented foods
note: Consume a variety daily: sauerkraut, kimchi, yogurt, kefir, miso, tempeh, and kombucha. Supports microbiome diversity, gut barrier integrity, and immune education.

name: Sauerkraut
note: Fermented cabbage rich in beneficial bacteria and vitamin K2. Choose raw, unpasteurized refrigerated varieties — heat destroys live cultures. Start with 1-2 tablespoons per day.

name: Kimchi
note: Lacto-fermented Korean cabbage and vegetables. Rich in probiotics, vitamins C and K, and anti-inflammatory compounds. Contains Lactobacillus plantarum.

name: Miso
note: Fermented soybean paste rich in beneficial bacteria and umami compounds. Add to warm (not boiling) water to preserve live cultures. Also provides protein, B vitamins, and minerals.

name: Tempeh
note: Whole soybean cake fermented with Rhizopus mold. Higher protein and more digestible than tofu. Rich in manganese, phosphorus, and calcium. Phytic acid partially broken down by fermentation.

name: Kombucha
note: Fermented tea providing beneficial acids (acetic, gluconic), B vitamins, and live cultures. Choose low-sugar options (<5g per serving). Avoid on low-histamine protocols.

name: Dark chocolate
note: 70%+ cacao preferred. Rich in flavanols, magnesium, iron, and theobromine. Supports cardiovascular health and mood. Start with 1-2 squares (20-30g) and choose minimal-ingredient bars.

name: Green tea
note: Rich in EGCG catechins, L-theanine, and modest caffeine. Supports metabolic function, cardiovascular health, and cognitive focus. Steep at 70-80°C to preserve catechins and reduce bitterness.

name: Matcha
note: Whole leaf green tea ground to powder. Higher EGCG content than steeped tea. Provides L-theanine + caffeine for sustained, calm alertness. Use ceremonial grade for drinking.

name: Herbal teas
note: Various therapeutic benefits: chamomile for sleep and anxiety, ginger for nausea and digestion, peppermint for IBS, rooibos as antioxidant-rich caffeine-free base.

name: Turmeric (in food)
note: Add to curries, golden milk, smoothies, and eggs. Combine with black pepper and fat to maximize curcumin absorption. Anti-inflammatory properties complement a whole-food diet.

name: Ginger (in food)
note: Anti-inflammatory and digestive support. Use fresh or powdered in teas, stir-fries, smoothies, and soups. Effective for nausea, bloating, and blood sugar modulation.

name: Collagen (as food additive)
note: Add hydrolyzed collagen peptides to coffee, smoothies, soups, or baked goods. Supports gut lining, joint health, and skin. Pair with vitamin C-rich foods for synthesis.

name: Protein intake
note: Aim for 1.2-2.0g per kg body weight (higher end for active individuals, older adults, or recovery). Distribute across meals. Prioritize animal-based complete proteins.

name: Fiber intake
note: Target 25-35g daily from diverse sources: vegetables, fruits, legumes, and whole grains. Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber. Increase gradually with adequate hydration to prevent GI distress.

name: Hydration
note: Baseline target: half your body weight in ounces of water per day. Increase for exercise, heat, sauna, or fasting. Electrolytes enhance hydration efficacy, particularly sodium and potassium.

name: Electrolytes (from food)
note: Sodium from sea salt, potassium from avocado and banana, magnesium from leafy greens and nuts, calcium from dairy or bones. Whole food sources preferred over supplementation when possible.

name: Omega-3 rich foods
note: Best sources: fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel), walnuts, flaxseed, chia seeds, hemp seeds. Aim for at least 2-3 servings of fatty fish per week for DHA/EPA.

name: Iron-rich foods
note: Heme iron (red meat, organ meat, shellfish) is most bioavailable. Non-heme iron (lentils, spinach, tofu) enhanced by vitamin C and reduced by coffee, tea, and calcium. Key for premenopausal women.

name: Calcium-rich foods
note: Dairy products, sardines with bones, leafy greens (bok choy, kale), and almonds. Vitamin D, K2, and magnesium required for effective calcium utilization and bone deposition.

name: Magnesium-rich foods
note: Best food sources: dark leafy greens, pumpkin seeds, dark chocolate, avocado, black beans, and almonds. Most people are marginally deficient. Soil depletion has reduced food magnesium content.
